section.title: Circumcision and Passover at Gilgal
section.sectionRationale: Before a single battle is fought, God halts the army to attend to worship and covenant. The generation born in the wilderness is circumcised, receiving the covenant sign their fathers had neglected; then Israel keeps the Passover in the land for the first time, and the manna ceases as they eat the produce of Canaan. It is one movement, the covenant renewal that must come before the conquest, teaching that the land is entered as a covenant people, not merely a conquering one.
section.thesis: God will not let Israel go to war until they are marked as His covenant people and have remembered His redemption, so He rolls away the reproach of Egypt through circumcision and Passover, and quietly ends the wilderness era as the manna stops and the promised land begins to feed them.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the kings' hearts melt and the new generation is circumcised, verses 1 to 9; the Passover kept in the land, verses 10 to 11; and the manna ceasing as Israel eats the produce of Canaan, verse 12.

units.label: The new generation circumcised
units.note: today I have rolled away the reproach of Egypt

verses.text: Now when all the Amorite kings west of the Jordan and all the Canaanite kings along the coast heard how the LORD had dried up the waters of the Jordan before the Israelites until they had crossed over, their hearts melted and their spirits failed for fear of the Israelites.
At that time the LORD said to Joshua, “Make flint knives and circumcise the sons of Israel once again.”
So Joshua made flint knives and circumcised the sons of Israel at Gibeath-haaraloth.
Now this is why Joshua circumcised them: All those who came out of Egypt, all the men of war, had died on the journey in the wilderness after they had left Egypt.
Though all who had come out were circumcised, none of those born in the wilderness on the journey from Egypt had been circumcised.

For the Israelites had wandered in the wilderness forty years, until all the nation’s men of war who had come out of Egypt had died, since they did not obey the LORD. So the LORD vowed never to let them see the land He had sworn to their fathers to give us, a land flowing with milk and honey.
And He raised up their sons in their place, and these were the ones Joshua circumcised. Until this time they were still uncircumcised, since they had not been circumcised along the way.
And after all the nation had been circumcised, they stayed there in the camp until they were healed.
Then the LORD said to Joshua, “Today I have rolled away the {{cherpah|reproach}} of Egypt from you.” So that place has been called Gilgal to this day.

units.label: The Passover kept in the land
units.note: they kept the Passover on the plains of Jericho

verses.text: On the evening of the fourteenth day of the month, while the Israelites were camped at Gilgal on the plains of Jericho, they kept the {{pesach|Passover}}.
The day after the Passover, on that very day, they ate unleavened bread and roasted grain from the produce of the land.

units.label: The manna ceases
units.note: that year they ate the crops of Canaan

verses.text: And the day after they had eaten from the produce of the land, the {{man|manna}} ceased. There was no more manna for the Israelites, so that year they began to eat the crops of the land of Canaan.

cherpah.gloss: Reproach, disgrace, scorn. The LORD says He has rolled away the reproach of Egypt from Israel. The reproach may be the shame of slavery and of the wilderness generation's unbelief, or the taunt that God brought them out only to let them die; renewing the covenant sign of circumcision removes it, marking them afresh as God's own people, no longer a byword but a nation set apart to Him.

pesach.gloss: Passover, from the root to pass over. The feast commemorating the night the LORD passed over the blood-marked houses in Egypt and spared Israel's firstborn, Exodus 12. Kept now in the land for the first time, it roots the conquest in redemption: the people who take Canaan are the people God redeemed by the blood of the lamb, and the feast points forward to Christ our Passover, 1 Corinthians 5:7.

man.gloss: Manna, the bread from heaven that fed Israel through forty years in the wilderness, Exodus 16. Its ceasing marks the end of the wilderness era: God's provision does not stop but changes form, from miraculous desert bread to the ordinary produce of the promised land. The God who fed them by miracle now feeds them through the harvest of the inheritance He gave.

basic.context: Israel has crossed the Jordan and the Canaanite kings are paralyzed with fear, yet God does not send Israel straight into battle. Instead He tells Joshua to attend to the covenant first. The whole generation born during the forty years in the wilderness had never been circumcised, the sign God gave Abraham that marked Israel as His people. So they are circumcised, and God says He has rolled away the reproach of Egypt from them. Then, healed and marked, they keep the Passover in the land for the first time, remembering the exodus. The very next day they eat the produce of Canaan, and the manna that had fed them for forty years stops. The question underneath is: what kind of people does God bring into His promises?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording covenant acts. Circumcision was the old covenant sign given to Abraham; the New Testament teaches that it pointed to a deeper circumcision of the heart, and that believers are marked out now not by the flesh but by faith and the Spirit, so we do not reinstate the physical rite. What endures is the truth the passage dramatizes: God's people belong to Him by covenant and are grounded in redemption before they are sent to serve.
basic.originalLanguages: God rolls away the cherpah, the reproach, of Egypt through the covenant sign. Israel keeps the pesach, the Passover, in the land, rooting the conquest in redemption. And the man, the manna, ceases as the promised land begins to feed them, the wilderness era quietly ending.
basic.typology: Circumcision points to the circumcision of the heart accomplished in Christ, and Passover points to Christ our Passover Lamb, whose blood spares us from judgment. The manna, bread from heaven, points to Jesus, the true bread from heaven who gives life to the world. All three signs at Gilgal find their fulfillment in Him.
basic.christ: Israel keeps the Passover just as they are about to receive the land, grounding everything in the blood of the lamb; Christ is our Passover, sacrificed for us. The manna that sustained them points to Jesus, who says, I am the bread of life. And the circumcision of the flesh points to the true circumcision, the putting off of the flesh, that comes through Christ.
basic.god: God is the covenant Lord who insists on being related to as such. He is patient, raising up a new generation after the old fell in unbelief, and gracious, rolling away their reproach. He is the faithful provider, changing manna for harvest without ceasing to feed His people. And He is the redeemer whose Passover stands at the foundation of all He does with Israel.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 17 gives circumcision as the covenant sign to Abraham. Exodus 12 institutes the Passover. Exodus 16 records the manna in the wilderness. Colossians 2 speaks of the circumcision made without hands in Christ. And John 6 presents Jesus as the true bread from heaven.
basic.oneStory: Before the conquest, God renews His covenant with Israel, marking them as His own and grounding them in the redemption of the Passover, while the wilderness manna gives way to the fruit of the promised land. Each sign, circumcision, Passover, and bread from heaven, points forward to Christ, in whom God's people are truly marked, redeemed, and fed.

context.literary: Chapter 5 forms a hinge between the crossing and the conquest. Verse 1 restates the melting fear of the Canaanite kings, echoing Rahab's words in chapter 2 and setting the stage. Then the narrative slows for three covenant acts, circumcision, Passover, and the ceasing of the manna, before the sudden appearance of the Commander of the LORD's army closes the chapter and opens the battle for Jericho. The naming of Gilgal, tied to the Hebrew for roll, ties the place to God rolling away the reproach.
context.historical: Circumcision was the sign of the Abrahamic covenant, Genesis 17, marking a male as a member of God's people. The wilderness generation had apparently suspended it during the years of judgment and wandering, so an entire generation needed it before they could keep the Passover, which the uncircumcised could not share, Exodus 12:48. Flint knives were traditional for the rite. The Passover fell on the fourteenth of the first month, and eating the land's produce, with the manna stopping, marked the definitive end of the forty-year wilderness provision. Gilgal became Israel's headquarters for the campaigns that followed.
context.canonical: The chapter binds the conquest to the exodus: the same God who redeemed Israel by the Passover now brings them into the land, and the covenant sign given to Abraham is renewed at the threshold of the promise. The three signs anticipate their fulfillment in the New Testament, the circumcision of the heart, Christ our Passover, and Jesus the bread of life, showing that the realities pictured here are finally about Christ and the people He redeems and marks as His own.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that God grounds the conquest in covenant and redemption, renewing the sign of circumcision on the new generation, restoring the Passover in the land, and marking the end of the wilderness era, so that Israel enters the promise as a redeemed, covenant people rather than merely as an army.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The physical rite of circumcision and the Mosaic Passover belong to the old covenant and are not reinstated for the church, which the New Testament marks by faith, baptism, and the Lord's Supper. What endures is the pattern: God relates to His people by covenant, roots their service in His prior redemption, and provides for them faithfully, whether by miracle or by ordinary means.

debates.question: What is the reproach of Egypt that God rolled away at Gilgal?

views.label: The shame of slavery and the wilderness generation's unbelief
views.case: Israel had been slaves in Egypt and then a nation under judgment, dying in the desert for unbelief and lacking even the covenant sign; renewing circumcision and keeping Passover in the land removes that whole history of disgrace, marking a fresh start for the redeemed people now entering the promise.

views.label: The taunt that God could not finish what He began
views.case: The reproach may be the mockery of the nations, that the LORD brought Israel out of Egypt only to kill them in the wilderness, Numbers 14:16; by bringing this generation across the Jordan and re-establishing the covenant, God silences that taunt and vindicates His faithfulness.

debates.bearing: Either way, the rolling away of the reproach declares that God has decisively restored His people to covenant standing and is fulfilling His promise, so they enter the land not in shame but as those He has claimed and vindicated.

study.originalLanguages: Three key words carry the meaning of this pause before the war. First, God says He has rolled away the cherpah, the reproach, of Egypt, the disgrace of slavery, of the unbelieving generation, and of the taunt that the LORD had failed His people; the renewal of the covenant sign wipes it clean, and the place is named Gilgal, tied to the Hebrew for rolling. Second, Israel keeps the pesach, the Passover, in the land for the first time, the feast that commemorates the night the LORD passed over the blood-marked houses and spared Israel's firstborn; keeping it now roots the conquest in redemption, so the people who take Canaan are precisely the people God bought with the blood of the lamb, and the feast points ahead to Christ our Passover, 1 Corinthians 5:7. Third, the man, the manna, the miraculous bread that fed them for forty wilderness years, ceases the moment they eat the produce of Canaan; God's provision does not fail but changes form, and the manna itself points to Jesus, the true bread from heaven, John 6:32-35. Reproach rolled away, Passover kept, manna ended: the vocabulary marks the close of the wilderness and the covenant beginning of life in the land.

typology.type: Circumcision, the covenant sign in the flesh
typology.fulfillment: The circumcision of the heart, made without hands, the putting off of the flesh through Christ, Colossians 2:11 and Romans 2:28-29.

typology.type: The Passover kept as Israel enters the land
typology.fulfillment: Christ our Passover, sacrificed for us, whose blood spares His people from judgment, 1 Corinthians 5:7 and 1 Peter 1:18-19.

typology.type: The manna, bread from heaven, ceasing as the land provides
typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the true bread from heaven who gives life to the world and never ceases, John 6:32-35 and 6:48-51.

study.christ: Every sign at Gilgal points to Jesus. Israel keeps the Passover on the very threshold of the promise, so that the whole conquest rests on the blood of the lamb, and Paul says plainly, Christ our Passover has been sacrificed; therefore let us keep the feast, 1 Corinthians 5:7. The circumcision that marks the new generation as God's covenant people points to the circumcision made without hands, the true putting off of the old sinful nature, which believers receive in Christ, Colossians 2:11. And the manna that fed Israel through the wilderness, now ceasing as the land bears fruit, foreshadows the one who says, I am the bread of life; your fathers ate the manna in the wilderness and died, but whoever eats this bread will live forever, John 6:48-51. Before Israel fights a single battle, God grounds them in redemption, covenant, and provision, all of which are finally found in Christ, the true Passover, the true circumcision of the heart, and the true bread from heaven.
study.god: The God of this passage will not be treated merely as a war-god who grants victories; He insists on covenant. Though the Canaanite kings are already paralyzed and the moment seems ripe to strike, He halts the army to renew the sign of belonging and to remember redemption. He is patient and faithful, having borne with a rebellious generation and now raising up their sons to inherit the promise He swore to the fathers. He is gracious, rolling away the reproach of His people and giving them a clean, fresh standing. And He is the constant provider, ending the manna only because the harvest of the land He gave is now theirs; His care never lapses, it only changes form. This is a God who redeems His people, claims them as His own, and feeds them at every stage of the journey.

crossReferences.note: God gives circumcision to Abraham as the sign of the covenant, the sign renewed here on the new generation.
Only the circumcised may keep the Passover, which is why the generation had to be circumcised before the feast.
Christ our Passover has been sacrificed for us; the feast Israel keeps points to Him.
Jesus is the true bread from heaven; the ceasing of the manna points to Him who gives eternal life.
In Christ believers receive a circumcision without hands, the true covenant sign of the heart.

study.oneStory: On the edge of the conquest God pauses the army to renew His covenant, marking the new generation with circumcision, restoring the Passover in the land, and ending the wilderness manna as the promised land begins to feed them. Israel enters the promise not merely as conquerors but as a redeemed, covenant people. Each sign, the covenant mark, the Passover lamb, and the bread from heaven, points forward to Christ, in whom God's people are truly marked as His own, redeemed by His blood, and fed with the bread of life.

quiz.q: Why did the new generation of Israel need to be circumcised at Gilgal?

options.text: Those born in the wilderness had never received the covenant sign, so they were marked as God's people before entering the land.
options.note: Verses 4 to 7 explain the whole wilderness generation was uncircumcised until now.

options.text: Circumcision was a medical requirement before battle.
options.note: It had nothing to do with medicine; it was the covenant sign given to Abraham.

options.text: It was a punishment for the sins of their fathers.
options.note: It was not a punishment but the restoration of the covenant sign to the new generation.

options.text: The Canaanites demanded it as a condition of surrender.
options.note: The Canaanites had no part in it; it was God's command to mark His covenant people.

quiz.q: What did God say He had done for Israel at Gilgal?

options.text: He had rolled away the reproach of Egypt from them.
options.note: Verse 9 gives this as the meaning of the covenant renewal and the name Gilgal.

options.text: He had given them permanent peace from all enemies.
options.note: Battles still lay ahead; what God did here was roll away their reproach, not end all war.

options.text: He had made them wealthier than the Canaanites.
options.note: The point was covenant standing and removed disgrace, not wealth.

options.text: He had cancelled the covenant made with Abraham.
options.note: The opposite: He renewed the covenant sign, He did not cancel the covenant.

quiz.q: What significant change happened to Israel's food supply?

options.text: The manna ceased once they ate the produce of the promised land.
options.note: Verse 12 marks the end of the wilderness manna as the land begins to feed them.

options.text: God sent a double portion of manna to celebrate the crossing.
options.note: The manna did not increase; it stopped, as the land's harvest took its place.

options.text: They ran out of food entirely and had to fast.
options.note: They did not go hungry; they simply ate the crops of Canaan instead of manna.

options.text: They returned to eating the food of Egypt.
options.note: They ate the produce of Canaan, the promised land, not the food of Egypt.

quiz.q: Why did God attend to circumcision and Passover before the fighting began?

options.text: So Israel would enter the land grounded in covenant and redemption, as God's people, not merely as an army.
options.note: The passage roots the conquest in covenant renewal and the Passover before any battle.

options.text: So the soldiers would be too weak to disobey orders.
options.note: They did rest to heal, but the purpose was covenant and redemption, not weakening the troops.

options.text: Because the Canaanites had asked for a delay.
options.note: No delay was requested; God halted them to renew the covenant, not at the enemy's request.

options.text: Because God had changed His mind about giving them the land.
options.note: God had not changed His mind; He was preparing His covenant people to receive the promise.

quiz.q: How do the signs at Gilgal point to Christ?

options.text: Passover points to Christ our Passover, the manna to Jesus the bread of life, and circumcision to the circumcision of the heart in Him.
options.note: All three signs find fulfillment in Christ, 1 Corinthians 5:7, John 6, Colossians 2:11.

options.text: They show that God's people are saved by keeping rituals in the flesh.
options.note: The signs are real, but they point beyond themselves to Christ; salvation is by faith in Him, not the rites themselves.

options.text: They teach that physical circumcision is still required of Christians.
options.note: The New Testament fulfills circumcision in the heart through Christ; the physical rite is not reinstated.

options.text: They have no connection to Christ or the gospel.
options.note: Passover, manna, and circumcision all clearly point forward to Christ.

questions: God rooted the conquest in redemption by having Israel keep the Passover first. How does remembering that you belong to God by redemption shape the way you approach the tasks He gives you?
God rolled away the reproach of Egypt and gave His people a fresh start. What past shame or failure do you need to entrust to the God who rolls reproach away in Christ?
The manna stopped when the land began to provide, yet God's care never lapsed. Where are you tempted to think God has stopped providing, when really His provision has only changed form?
God halted a whole army to attend to covenant and worship before battle. Are there ways you rush into activity and service while neglecting your relationship with God?
Passover, manna, and circumcision all point to Christ, our Passover, our bread of life, and our true circumcision. How does seeing Jesus in these signs deepen your love for Him this week?

translationNotes.note: Circumcise the sons of Israel once again renders a phrase the BSB footnote gives literally as again the second time; it refers to renewing the practice for the new generation, not repeating it on individuals.
Gibeath-haaraloth means the hill of the foreskins, as the BSB footnote notes, naming the place of the circumcision.
Gilgal sounds like the Hebrew for roll, as the BSB footnote notes, tying the name to God rolling away the reproach of Egypt.
The BSB sets off all the men of war with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 5:1-12. Prose narrative. The em dashes in verse 4 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
